text: The surgeon adjusts the position to access the anterior leaflet, preparing to excise it.
The surgeon opens up the side of the surgical field, potentially revealing the vagus nerve.
The surgeon pulls up the fibers and spreads them laterally to create space, focusing on the angle of the fibers.
The surgeon removes tissue from the left crus, using precise dissection techniques to separate and excise the targeted structures.

The surgeon inspects the left side for any bleeding, noting no significant bleeding present. The short gastric vessels are then taken down, and preparations are made to mobilize the esophagus by opening the hiatus and bringing the esophagus down further.
The surgeon injects the esophagus with a solution of epinephrine, aiming to reduce bleeding and create a wheal that helps separate the veins. A small vein is visible over the top of the esophagus.
The surgeon begins spreading the muscle fibers, focusing on the upper muscle area where the fibers are visible. The mediastinum is open, and the CO₂ levels are rising.
The surgeon identifies the z-line, a visible demarcation in the esophagus, and discusses the need to move slightly further down, approximately another centimeter, to reach the desired location.
